questiontext.text: Which of the following defines a metric on \(\mathbb{R}^2\)? (All options use points \(x=(x_1,x_2)\) and \(y=(y_1,y_2)\).)

answer.text: \(d(x,y) = \max(|x_1-y_1|, |x_2-y_2|)\) (the \(\ell^\infty\) metric).

feedback.text: Correct. The \(\ell^\infty\) (sup-norm) metric satisfies all metric axioms: d(x,y)≥0, d(x,y)=0⟺x=y, symmetry d(x,y)=d(y,x), and the triangle inequality d(x,z)≤max(|x₁−z₁|,|x₂−z₂|)≤d(x,y)+d(y,z).

answer.text: \(d(x,y) = (x_1-y_1)^2 + (x_2-y_2)^2\) (sum of squares, without square root).

feedback.text: This fails the triangle inequality. For example with x=(1,0), y=(0,1), z=(0,0): d(x,z)+d(z,y)=1+1=2 but d(x,y)=(−1)²+(−1)²=2. Actually this is borderline, but for x=(2,0),z=(1,0),y=(0,0): d(x,z)=1, d(z,y)=1, d(x,y)=4 > 2. Triangle inequality fails.
This can be negative (e.g., x=(0,0), y=(0,1): d=0−1=−1<0), violating non-negativity. Not a metric.
This can be negative if x₁+y₁<0, violating non-negativity. Not a metric in general.

questiontext.text: Which of the following metric spaces is NOT complete?

answer.text: \((\mathbb{Q}, |\cdot|)\): the rationals with the usual absolute value metric.

feedback.text: Correct. ℚ is not complete: the sequence of rational approximations to √2 (e.g., 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, ...) is Cauchy in ℚ but converges to √2 ∉ ℚ. The completion of ℚ is ℝ.

answer.text: \((\mathbb{R}, |\cdot|)\): the reals with the usual metric.

feedback.text: ℝ with the usual metric is complete (every Cauchy sequence converges in ℝ). This is the fundamental property of the real number system.

answer.text: \((C[0,1], \|\cdot\|_\infty)\): continuous functions with the sup-norm.

feedback.text: C[0,1] with the sup-norm is complete (the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous). This is a classic example of a complete metric (Banach) space.

answer.text: \((\mathbb{Z}, |\cdot|)\): the integers with the usual metric.

feedback.text: ℤ is complete (every Cauchy sequence of integers is eventually constant, hence convergent). Any discrete metric space is complete.

questiontext.text: The Baire category theorem states: a complete metric space is of the second category, meaning:

answer.text: It cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere dense closed sets.

feedback.text: Correct. A set is nowhere dense if its closure has empty interior. Baire's theorem: a nonempty complete metric space is NOT a countable union of nowhere dense sets (it is of the "second Baire category"). Key applications: closed graph theorem, uniform boundedness principle.

answer.text: Every open set is dense.

feedback.text: Not every open set is dense (open sets can be small). The Baire category theorem is about countable unions of nowhere dense sets, not individual open sets.

answer.text: It is separable (has a countable dense subset).

feedback.text: Separability (countable dense subset) is a different property. Complete metric spaces need not be separable (e.g., \(\ell^\infty\) is complete but not separable).

answer.text: Every Cauchy sequence converges.

feedback.text: This is the definition of completeness, not the Baire category theorem. The Baire category theorem is a consequence of completeness, stating that complete spaces are of the second category.

questiontext.text: A subset \(K\) of a metric space \((X,d)\) is compact if and only if:

answer.text: Every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence converging to a point in \(K\) (sequentially compact).

feedback.text: Correct. In metric spaces, compactness, sequential compactness, and "complete + totally bounded" are all equivalent. Every sequence in a compact metric space has a convergent subsequence (Bolzano-Weierstrass property).

answer.text: \(K\) is closed and bounded.

feedback.text: Closed and bounded implies compact in ℝⁿ (Heine-Borel theorem) but NOT in general metric spaces. For example, the closed unit ball in an infinite-dimensional normed space is closed and bounded but not compact.

answer.text: \(K\) is open and connected.

feedback.text: Open connected sets (like intervals) are generally not compact. Compact sets in Hausdorff spaces are closed, not open (unless the space is discrete).

answer.text: \(K\) is a finite set.

feedback.text: Finite sets are compact but compactness does not require finiteness. Infinite compact metric spaces exist (e.g., [0,1] in ℝ).

questiontext.text: The Banach fixed-point theorem (contraction mapping principle) states: if \((X,d)\) is a complete metric space and \(T: X \to X\) satisfies \(d(Tx,Ty) \leq k\,d(x,y)\) for some \(k \in [0,1)\), then:

answer.text: \(T\) has a unique fixed point \(x^* = Tx^*\), and the iteration \(x_{n+1} = Tx_n\) converges to \(x^*\) with \(d(x_n, x^*) \leq \dfrac{k^n}{1-k}d(x_1,x_0)\).

feedback.text: Correct. The Banach contraction principle guarantees existence and uniqueness of a fixed point, with the iterative sequence converging geometrically. The error bound k^n/(1−k)·d(x₁,x₀) gives an a priori estimate.

answer.text: \(T\) may have no fixed point if \(X\) is not compact.

feedback.text: Completeness (not compactness) is the key hypothesis. In a complete metric space, any contraction has a unique fixed point regardless of compactness.

answer.text: \(T\) has at least one fixed point but may have more.

feedback.text: The contraction condition ensures UNIQUENESS of the fixed point. If x*=Tx* and y*=Ty*, then d(x*,y*)=d(Tx*,Ty*)≤k·d(x*,y*), so (1−k)d(x*,y*)≤0, meaning d(x*,y*)=0 and x*=y*.

answer.text: The fixed point exists only if \(k < 1/2\).

feedback.text: The theorem works for any k∈[0,1). The tighter k is, the faster the convergence, but any k<1 is sufficient.

questiontext.text: The Cantor set \(C\) (constructed by repeatedly removing middle thirds from \([0,1]\)) has the following properties:

answer.text: \(C\) is closed, perfect (every point is a limit point), nowhere dense, has Lebesgue measure 0, and has cardinality \(|\mathbb{R}|\) (uncountable).

feedback.text: Correct. The Cantor set C is: (1) closed (complement is open), (2) perfect (no isolated points), (3) nowhere dense (interior is empty), (4) measure zero (removed intervals have total length 1), (5) uncountable (homeomorphic to {0,1}^ℕ via ternary expansions).

answer.text: \(C\) is open, dense in \([0,1]\), and has Lebesgue measure 1.

feedback.text: Exactly opposite: C is closed (not open), nowhere dense (not dense), and has measure 0 (not 1). The complementary open set (union of removed intervals) is dense and has measure 1.

answer.text: \(C\) is countably infinite (contains all rationals in \([0,1]\) whose ternary expansion uses only 0 and 2).

feedback.text: The Cantor set consists of ALL numbers in [0,1] whose ternary expansion uses only 0 and 2 (not just rationals). This set is uncountable, not countable.

answer.text: \(C\) has positive Lebesgue measure \(1/3\).

feedback.text: The standard Cantor set has measure 0 (at step n, measure = (2/3)^n → 0). A "fat" Cantor set can have positive measure, but the standard middle-thirds Cantor set has measure 0.

questiontext.text: A metric space \(X\) is connected if and only if:

answer.text: The only subsets of \(X\) that are both open and closed (clopen) are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) itself.

feedback.text: Correct. X is connected iff it cannot be partitioned into two nonempty disjoint open sets — equivalently, the only clopen sets are ∅ and X. Connected subsets of ℝ are exactly the intervals (including singleton sets and ℝ itself).

answer.text: Every two points in \(X\) can be connected by a path.

feedback.text: Path-connectedness implies connectedness but not conversely. The topologist's sine curve {(x,sin(1/x)):x>0}∪{(0,0)} is connected but not path-connected.

answer.text: \(X\) is complete and separable.

feedback.text: Completeness and separability are metric properties unrelated to connectedness. ℝ is connected, separable, and complete, but ℚ is separable and connected (as a topological space?) — wait, ℚ is actually totally disconnected.

answer.text: \(X\) has no isolated points.

feedback.text: Having no isolated points (perfect space) does not imply connectedness. The Cantor set is perfect but totally disconnected.

questiontext.text: The Arzelà-Ascoli theorem characterizes compactness in \(C(K)\) (continuous functions on a compact metric space \(K\)). A subset \(\mathcal{F} \subset C(K)\) is relatively compact (has compact closure) if and only if:

answer.text: \(\mathcal{F}\) is uniformly bounded (\(\sup_f\|f\|_\infty < \infty\)) and equicontinuous (\(\forall \varepsilon > 0\; \exists \delta > 0\) such that \(d(x,y)<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon\) for all \(f \in \mathcal{F}\)).

feedback.text: Correct. Arzelà-Ascoli: relative compactness in C(K) is equivalent to uniform boundedness + equicontinuity. Every sequence in ℱ then has a uniformly convergent subsequence.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{F}\) is closed and bounded in \(C(K)\).

feedback.text: In infinite-dimensional spaces (like C(K)), closed and bounded does NOT imply compact (Riesz's lemma). Equicontinuity is the additional condition needed for Arzelà-Ascoli.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{F}\) consists of Lipschitz functions with a uniform Lipschitz constant.

feedback.text: Uniform Lipschitz implies equicontinuous, and if also uniformly bounded, then Arzelà-Ascoli applies. But the theorem's condition is the broader equicontinuity, not just Lipschitz.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{F}\) is a convex subset of \(C(K)\).

feedback.text: Convexity has no bearing on compactness in C(K). Arzelà-Ascoli requires uniform boundedness and equicontinuity, not convexity.

questiontext.text: The completion \(\widetilde{X}\) of a metric space \((X,d)\) is:

answer.text: A complete metric space containing an isometric copy of \(X\) as a dense subspace, unique up to isometry.

feedback.text: Correct. Every metric space has a completion, constructed as equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences. X embeds isometrically as a dense subset of its completion (e.g., ℚ completes to ℝ). The completion is unique up to isometry.

answer.text: The set of all bounded subsets of \(X\).

feedback.text: The power set of bounded subsets is not the completion. The completion consists of equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences, not subsets.

answer.text: The closure of \(X\) in any surrounding metric space.

feedback.text: The closure of X in a surrounding space depends on how X is embedded. The intrinsic completion is uniquely defined by the metric of X itself.

answer.text: The one-point compactification of \(X\).

feedback.text: One-point compactification adds a "point at infinity" to make a locally compact space compact — this is different from completion (adding limits of Cauchy sequences).

questiontext.text: In a metric space, which statement is always true?

answer.text: An open ball \(B(x,r) = \{y : d(x,y) < r\}\) is an open set, and a closed ball \(\overline{B}(x,r) = \{y : d(x,y) \leq r\}\) is a closed set.

feedback.text: Correct. Open balls are open (for any z∈B(x,r), the ball B(z,r−d(x,z)) ⊆ B(x,r)) and closed balls are closed (their complements are open). These are foundational properties of metric topology.

answer.text: The closure of an open ball equals the corresponding closed ball: \(\overline{B(x,r)} = \overline{B}(x,r)\).

feedback.text: False in general metric spaces. In a discrete metric, B(x,1)={x} and its closure is {x}, but \(\overline{B}(x,1)=X\). Equality holds in ℝⁿ but not in all metric spaces.

answer.text: Every closed set is compact.

feedback.text: False: closed sets need not be compact. In ℝ, ℝ itself is closed but not compact. In general metric spaces, closed + bounded ≠ compact.

answer.text: Every open set is connected.

feedback.text: False: open sets need not be connected. For example, (0,1)∪(2,3) is open in ℝ but disconnected.

questiontext.text: A function \(f: (X,d_X) \to (Y,d_Y)\) between metric spaces is uniformly continuous if:

answer.text: \(\forall\varepsilon > 0\; \exists\delta > 0\) such that \(d_X(x,x') < \delta \Rightarrow d_Y(f(x),f(x')) < \varepsilon\) for ALL \(x,x' \in X\).

feedback.text: Correct. The key difference from pointwise continuity: the same δ works for ALL pairs (x,x'), not just for each fixed x. Uniform continuity is guaranteed on compact domains (a continuous function on a compact metric space is uniformly continuous — Heine-Cantor theorem).

answer.text: \(\forall x \in X\; \forall\varepsilon > 0\; \exists\delta > 0\) such that \(d_X(x,x') < \delta \Rightarrow d_Y(f(x),f(x')) < \varepsilon\).

feedback.text: This is pointwise continuity, not uniform continuity. In uniform continuity, δ is chosen independently of x (uniform over all x). Pointwise continuity allows δ to depend on x.

answer.text: \(f\) preserves distances: \(d_Y(f(x),f(y)) = d_X(x,y)\) for all \(x,y\).

feedback.text: This is an isometry (distance-preserving map), a strictly stronger condition than uniform continuity.

answer.text: \(f\) is differentiable and \(f'\) is bounded.

feedback.text: Bounded derivative implies Lipschitz which implies uniform continuity, but uniform continuity does not require differentiability. For example, x^{1/2} on [0,1] is uniformly continuous but not Lipschitz at 0.

questiontext.text: A metric space is separable if and only if:

answer.text: It has a countable dense subset (e.g., \(\mathbb{Q}\) is dense in \(\mathbb{R}\), so \(\mathbb{R}\) is separable).

feedback.text: Correct. A separable metric space has a countable set D such that D̄=X. Examples: ℝⁿ (dense: ℚⁿ), C[0,1] (dense: polynomials with rational coefficients), ℓ² (dense: sequences with finitely many nonzero rational terms). Note: ℓ∞ is NOT separable.

answer.text: It satisfies the second axiom of countability (has a countable base).

feedback.text: In metric spaces, separability and second countability are equivalent. However, stating this as a definition is circular (the question asks for the definition of separability).

answer.text: It is compact.

feedback.text: Compact metric spaces are separable, but not conversely. ℝ is separable but not compact.

answer.text: It can be embedded isometrically in \(\mathbb{R}\).

feedback.text: Isometric embedding in ℝ would force the space to be a subspace of ℝ (hence separable), but not all separable metric spaces embed isometrically in ℝ (e.g., ℝ² cannot embed isometrically in ℝ).

questiontext.text: The function \(f(x) = \sqrt{x}\) on \([0,1]\) is:

answer.text: Uniformly continuous but not Lipschitz continuous on \([0,1]\).

feedback.text: Correct. f is uniformly continuous on [0,1] (it is continuous on a compact set). However, |f(x)−f(0)|/|x−0| = 1/√x → ∞ as x→0⁺, so f is not Lipschitz at x=0. Uniform continuity holds without Lipschitz.

answer.text: Lipschitz continuous with constant \(L = 1/2\).

feedback.text: If Lipschitz, then |√x − √y| ≤ L|x−y| for all x,y∈[0,1]. But |√x−0|/|x−0| = 1/√x → ∞ as x→0, so no finite L works. f is not Lipschitz on [0,1].

answer.text: Neither continuous nor Lipschitz on \([0,1]\).

feedback.text: √x is continuous (in fact uniformly continuous) on [0,1]. It is the Lipschitz condition that fails, not continuity.

answer.text: Both uniformly continuous and Lipschitz on \([0,1]\).

feedback.text: √x is uniformly continuous but NOT Lipschitz on [0,1] (the Lipschitz constant blows up at x=0). Lipschitz would require |√x−√y| ≤ L|x−y| for some fixed L, which fails near 0.

questiontext.text: Two metric spaces \((X,d_X)\) and \((Y,d_Y)\) are isometric if:

answer.text: There exists a bijection \(f: X \to Y\) such that \(d_Y(f(x),f(x')) = d_X(x,x')\) for all \(x,x' \in X\).

feedback.text: Correct. An isometry is a distance-preserving bijection. Isometric spaces are metrically identical: all metric properties (completeness, compactness, diameter, etc.) are preserved under isometry.

answer.text: There exists a continuous bijection \(f: X \to Y\) (a homeomorphism).

feedback.text: A homeomorphism preserves topological properties but not metric ones. For instance, (0,1) and ℝ are homeomorphic (via tan(π(x−1/2))) but not isometric (one is bounded, the other is not).

answer.text: \(X\) and \(Y\) have the same cardinality.

feedback.text: Same cardinality alone is far too weak. For example, [0,1] and [0,2] have the same cardinality (both uncountable) but are not isometric (different diameters).

answer.text: There is a surjective map \(f: X \to Y\) with \(d_Y(f(x),f(y)) \leq d_X(x,y)\) (short map).

feedback.text: A short map (non-expansive surjection) is weaker than an isometry. An isometry requires exact equality d_Y(f(x),f(y))=d_X(x,y) AND bijectivity.

questiontext.text: The Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) states:

answer.text: Every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) has a convergent subsequence.

feedback.text: Correct. In ℝⁿ, bounded sequences are contained in a compact set (by Heine-Borel: closed bounded sets in ℝⁿ are compact), so every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence. This is one of the most fundamental theorems in analysis.

answer.text: Every Cauchy sequence in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) converges.

feedback.text: This is the completeness of ℝⁿ, not the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem. BW is specifically about bounded sequences having convergent subsequences (which need not be Cauchy).

answer.text: Every infinite bounded set in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) has an accumulation point.

feedback.text: This is also a valid form of Bolzano-Weierstrass (for sets rather than sequences), and it is equivalent to the stated version. Both formulations are correct; the question asks which option IS the theorem.

answer.text: Every open cover of a bounded set in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) has a finite subcover.

feedback.text: This is the Heine-Borel theorem for closed bounded sets in ℝⁿ (which must also be closed to be compact). Bolzano-Weierstrass is about sequential compactness, not open covers.

questiontext.text: In the metric space \((C[0,1], \|\cdot\|_\infty)\), the set of polynomials is:

answer.text: Dense, by the Weierstrass approximation theorem.

feedback.text: Correct. The Weierstrass approximation theorem states that every continuous function on [0,1] can be uniformly approximated by polynomials. Thus the polynomials (or polynomials with rational coefficients) form a countable dense subset of C[0,1], proving C[0,1] is separable.

answer.text: Closed and not dense.

feedback.text: The set of polynomials is NOT closed in C[0,1] (its closure is all of C[0,1] by the Weierstrass theorem). Hence it is dense, not closed.

answer.text: Open in \(C[0,1]\).

feedback.text: The set of polynomials is not open in C[0,1]: no open ball around a polynomial contains only polynomials (small perturbations of a polynomial can be non-polynomial continuous functions).

answer.text: A closed nowhere dense set (of the first Baire category).

feedback.text: Polynomials form a dense set (not nowhere dense). The Weierstrass theorem ensures their closure is all of C[0,1].

questiontext.text: If \(\{F_n\}\) is a decreasing sequence of nonempty closed sets in a complete metric space with \(\mathrm{diam}(F_n) \to 0\), then:

answer.text: \(\displaystyle\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty F_n\) consists of exactly one point.

feedback.text: Correct. This is Cantor's intersection theorem for complete metric spaces: F₁⊇F₂⊇⋯ are closed, nonempty, and diam(Fₙ)→0 implies ∩Fₙ is a singleton. The proof picks xₙ∈Fₙ; since diam(Fₙ)→0, {xₙ} is Cauchy, converges to a unique limit in ∩Fₙ.

answer.text: \(\displaystyle\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty F_n\) is empty.

feedback.text: Without completeness, ∩Fₙ could be empty (e.g., Fₙ=(0,1/n) in ℚ or ℝ⧵{0}). But in a COMPLETE metric space with diam(Fₙ)→0, the intersection is a singleton.

answer.text: \(\displaystyle\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty F_n\) contains infinitely many points.

feedback.text: Since diam(Fₙ)→0, the intersection (if nonempty) can contain at most one point (two distinct points would have positive distance, contradicting diam→0). Combined with nonemptiness, it is exactly one point.

answer.text: The result fails without the assumption \(\mathrm{diam}(F_n) \to 0\).

feedback.text: True, but this is not the content of the theorem. The theorem states that WITH diam(Fₙ)→0 in a complete space, ∩Fₙ is a singleton. Without this assumption, ∩Fₙ could be empty or contain multiple points.

questiontext.text: A metric space \(X\) is totally bounded if:

answer.text: For every \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exist finitely many points \(x_1,\ldots,x_n \in X\) such that \(X = \bigcup_{i=1}^n B(x_i,\varepsilon)\).

feedback.text: Correct. Total boundedness means X can be covered by finitely many balls of any given radius. In metric spaces: compact ⟺ complete + totally bounded. Total boundedness implies bounded but not conversely (e.g., ℓ∞ unit ball is bounded but not totally bounded).

answer.text: The diameter \(\mathrm{diam}(X) < \infty\).

feedback.text: Being bounded (finite diameter) does not imply totally bounded. In infinite-dimensional normed spaces, the closed unit ball is bounded but not totally bounded.

answer.text: \(X\) is a finite set.

feedback.text: Finite sets are totally bounded, but totally bounded does not require finiteness. For example, [0,1]⊂ℝ is totally bounded (and infinite).

answer.text: \(X\) has a countable base.

feedback.text: Second countability (countable base) is a topological property separate from total boundedness. A metric space is second countable iff separable, which is not the same as totally bounded.

questiontext.text: If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous surjection and \(X\) is compact, then \(Y\) is:

answer.text: Compact (continuous images of compact sets are compact).

feedback.text: Correct. Compactness is preserved under continuous maps: if f is continuous and X is compact, then f(X) is compact. Since f is surjective, Y=f(X) is compact.

answer.text: Complete (continuous images of complete spaces are complete).

feedback.text: Completeness is NOT generally preserved by continuous maps. For example, f:(0,1)→(0,1) with f(x)=x; (0,1) with the usual metric is not complete, but even if X=ℝ (complete), f(ℝ)=(0,1) (not complete) under f(x)=e^x/(1+e^x).

answer.text: Separable but not necessarily compact.

feedback.text: A continuous surjective image of a compact space is compact (and compact metric spaces are separable), but the stronger conclusion is compactness, not just separability.

answer.text: Connected only if \(X\) is path-connected.

feedback.text: Continuous images of connected sets are connected. Since X is compact (hence connected if... wait, compact spaces need not be connected). Actually the correct statement is: if X is connected and f continuous, then f(X) is connected. But being compact does not imply connected.

questiontext.text: On \(\mathbb{R}^n\), which of the following metrics generates the SAME topology as the Euclidean metric \(d_2(x,y) = \|x-y\|_2\)?

answer.text: Both \(d_1(x,y) = \|x-y\|_1 = \sum|x_i-y_i|\) and \(d_\infty(x,y) = \max|x_i-y_i|\) generate the same topology as \(d_2\).

feedback.text: Correct. All \(\ell^p\) norms on ℝⁿ are equivalent: there exist constants C₁, C₂>0 such that C₁d_2 ≤ d_p ≤ C₂d_2. Equivalent metrics generate the same open sets (same topology), so convergence, continuity, compactness, and completeness are the same.

answer.text: \(d_1\) generates the same topology but \(d_\infty\) generates a different (finer) topology.

feedback.text: All \(\ell^p\) norms on finite-dimensional ℝⁿ are equivalent, generating the same topology. None is finer or coarser than another.

answer.text: The discrete metric \(d(x,y) = 1\) for \(x \neq y\) generates the same topology.

feedback.text: The discrete metric generates the discrete topology (every set is open), which is strictly finer than the Euclidean topology on ℝⁿ (e.g., singletons are not open in the Euclidean topology for n≥1).

answer.text: The metric \(d(x,y) = \min(1, \|x-y\|_2)\) generates a different topology.

feedback.text: The bounded metric d(x,y)=min(1,‖x−y‖₂) is equivalent to d₂ (it generates the same topology). They define the same open balls for small radii and hence the same topology.
